text: The plastic tab on a loaf of bread. Such a small, unassuming thing, and yet it solves a problem with perfect economy. It holds the bag closed without ceremony, costs almost nothing, and waits patiently in a drawer until needed again. Often it's printed with the day the bread was baked, so it carries a quiet little timestamp, a record of freshness pressed into a square no bigger than a thumbnail. I find something moving in how thoroughly it was thought through by someone I'll never meet. It asks for no attention and receives none, which is its own kind of grace. So many of the objects that hold our days together are like this: humble, exact, and content to go unnoticed. Beauty, I've come to think, isn't always in the grand or the rare. Sometimes it lives in the things that simply work, faithfully, and ask for nothing in return.
